**Ōtsu** (大津市, *Ōtsu-shi*) is the capital [city](/wiki/Cities_of_Japan "Cities of Japan") of [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). As of 1 October 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit), the city had an estimated population of 343,991 in 153,458 households and a [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of 740 persons per km2.[[1]](#cite_note-Ōtsu-hp-1) The total area of the city is 464.51 square kilometres (179.35 sq mi).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Ōtsu is part of ancient [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province") and has been settled since at least the [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period"). It was an important center of inland water transportation on Lake Biwa and was referred to in the [Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū") as Shiga no Ōwada (志賀の大わだ) and Shigatsu (志賀津).[[2]](#cite_note-c-2) It was also on the main land routes, the [Tōkaidō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_(road) "Tōkaidō (road)") and the [Nakasendō](/wiki/Nakasend%C5%8D "Nakasendō") connecting the eastern provinces with the ancient capitals of Japan.[[3]](#cite_note-e-3)[[4]](#cite_note-d-4) Additionally, the ancient [Hokurikudō](/wiki/Hokurikud%C5%8D "Hokurikudō"), which connected Kyoto to the provinces of northern [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), ran through Ōtsu.[[4]](#cite_note-d-4) From 667 to 672, the [Ōmi Ōtsu Palace](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_%C5%8Ctsu_Palace "Ōmi Ōtsu Palace"), founded by [Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Emperor_Tenji "Emperor Tenji"), was the capital of Japan.[[3]](#cite_note-e-3) Following the [Jinshin War](/wiki/Jinshin_War "Jinshin War") Ōtsu was renamed *Furutsu* (古津; "old port").[[2]](#cite_note-c-2) A new capital, [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō"), (now [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")), was established in the immediate neighborhood in 794, and Ōtsu (meaning "big port") was revived as an important traffic point and satellite town of the capital. With the establishment of the new capital, the name of the city was restored to "Ōtsu".[[5]](#cite_note-k-5)[[2]](#cite_note-c-2)

Ōtsu prospered during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") because of its port on Lake Biwa and as [Ōtsu-juku](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu-juku "Ōtsu-juku"), a major *[shukuba](/wiki/Shukuba "Shukuba")* on the Tōkaidō and Nakasendō highways. The city was under direct administration of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), both for its strategic location and for its role as a center of travel and trade.[[4]](#cite_note-d-4) [Zeze Domain](/wiki/Zeze_Domain "Zeze Domain") was based in Zeze, a neighboring [castle town](/wiki/J%C5%8Dkamachi "Jōkamachi"), and the smaller [Katada Domain](/w/index.php?title=Katada_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Katada Domain (page does not exist)") occupied the northern area of the present-day city from 1698 to 1826.[[6]](#cite_note-zeze-6)[[7]](#cite_note-katada-7)

### Modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern period")]

The [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") of 1868 saw the establishment of a new central government in Tokyo and the [abolition of the han system](/wiki/Abolition_of_the_han_system "Abolition of the han system"). Numerous [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") under control of the [Meiji government](/wiki/Meiji_government "Meiji government") were created, and part of the old province of [Ōmi](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province") was designated as [Ōtsu Prefecture](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu_Prefecture&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtsu Prefecture (page does not exist)") in 1868. Several smaller prefectures were merged into Ōtsu Prefecture in 1871, which became part of present-day Shiga Prefecture on January 1, 1872. Ōtsu was named the prefectural capital of Shiga.[[8]](#cite_note-jkn21-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The town of Ōtsu was established on 1 April 1889 with the creation of the modern municipalities system. It was raised to city status on 1 October 1898.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Street_in_%C5%8Ctsu_%281891%29.jpg/250px-Street_in_%C5%8Ctsu_%281891%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Street_in_%C5%8Ctsu_(1891).jpg)

The street in Ōtsu, where the attack on Nicholas II was carried out, 1891

The [Ōtsu incident](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu_incident "Ōtsu incident"), a failed [assassination](/wiki/Assassination "Assassination") attempt on [Tsarevich](/wiki/Tsarevich "Tsarevich") Nicholas Alexandrovich of [Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") (1868 – 1918, later Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II "Nicholas II")), occurred on 11 May 1891. Nicholas, returning to Kyoto after a day trip to [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa"), was attacked with a [saber](/wiki/Saber "Saber") by [Tsuda Sanzō](/wiki/Tsuda_Sanz%C5%8D "Tsuda Sanzō") (1855 – 1891), an escort policeman. Nicholas survived the assassination attempt, but the incident was seen as a crisis in Japanese-Russian relations.[[10]](#cite_note-jkn211-10)[[11]](#cite_note-jkn212-11) For a while the local populace considered renaming the city to avoid the stigma associated with the scandal, but the idea was eventually shelved.

The [Lake Biwa Canal](/wiki/Lake_Biwa_Canal "Lake Biwa Canal") (8.7 kilometres (5.4 mi)) was constructed in the 1890s between [Ōtsu and Kyoto](/wiki/Greater_Kyoto "Greater Kyoto"). The canal, which was later expanded during the [Taishō period](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_period "Taishō period"), played an important role in connecting the cities, facilitating water and passenger transportation, and providing electrical energy to power Japan's first [streetcar](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar") railroad services. The canal was [designated](/wiki/Cultural_Properties_of_Japan "Cultural Properties of Japan") a [Historic Site](/wiki/Historic_Sites_of_Japan "Historic Sites of Japan") in 1996.[[12]](#cite_note-bunka-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Lake_Biwa_Canal-13)

The city area gradually expanded by annexation of the village of Shiga in 1932, towns of Zeze and Ishiyama in 1933, villages of Sakamoto, Ogoto, Sakashita-honmachi, Oishi and Shimoda-kamimura in 1951, and towns of Katata and Seta in 1967. On March 20, 2006, the town of [Shiga](/wiki/Shiga,_Shiga "Shiga, Shiga") (from [Shiga District](/wiki/Shiga_District,_Shiga "Shiga District, Shiga")) ceased to exist after merging into Ōtsu.[[4]](#cite_note-d-4)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geography")]

Ōtsu is located on the southern and western shore of [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa") and occupies most of the southwestern portion of Shiga Prefecture.[[5]](#cite_note-k-5) The city is L-shaped and stretches along the southwest shore of [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa"), Japan's largest lake.[[4]](#cite_note-d-4) Ōtsu ranges from the densely populated [alluvium](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium") depressions near the shore of Lake Biwa to sparsely populated hilly and mountainous areas to the west ([Hira Mountains](/wiki/Hira_Mountains "Hira Mountains") and [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei")) and south of the city.[[5]](#cite_note-k-5) [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") to the west encompasses much of the border of the city and Shiga Prefecture with [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Climate")]

Ōtsu has a [Humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (Köppen *Cfa*) characterized by warm summers and cool winters with light to no snowfall. The average annual temperature in Ōtsu is 13.8 °C. The average annual rainfall is 1430 mm with September as the wettest month. The temperatures are highest on average in August, at around 25.8 °C, and lowest in January, at around 2.3 °C.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Government")]

Ōtsu has a [mayor-council](/wiki/Mayor-council "Mayor-council") form of government with a directly elected mayor and a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") city council of 38 members, who serve a term of four years. Ōtsu contributes two members to the Shiga Prefectural Assembly. In terms of national politics, the city is part of [Shiga 1st district](/wiki/Shiga_1st_district "Shiga 1st district") of the [lower house](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_Japan "House of Representatives of Japan") of the [Diet of Japan](/wiki/Diet_of_Japan "Diet of Japan"). The city hall of Ōtsu is located in the central Goryō-chō district of the city.[[17]](#cite_note-shiga4-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Basic_Facts_on_City_Council-18) The mayor of Ōtsu is Kenji Sato, who became the 24th mayor of the city in 2020.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Economy")]

Ōtsu was historically noted for the production of several products, including *[Ōtsu-e](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu-e&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtsu-e (page does not exist)")*, a form of folk drawing purchased by travelers in the Edo period; the *[Ōtsu soroban](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu_soroban&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtsu soroban (page does not exist)")*, an abacus used widely in Japan from the early 17th century; *[Zeze-yaki](/wiki/Zeze-yaki "Zeze-yaki")* and *[Konan-yaki](/w/index.php?title=Konan-yaki&action=edit&redlink=1 "Konan-yaki (page does not exist)"),* forms of [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") produced in the Edo period; and *Zeze-cha*, the first Japanese tea to be exported to the United States.[[19]](#cite_note-jkn213-19)[[20]](#cite_note-shiga-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Zeze-yaki-22)

Ōtsu, while not an agricultural city, is home to the production of edible [chrysanthemums](/wiki/Chrysanthemum "Chrysanthemum"), used in [Japanese cuisine](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine "Japanese cuisine") in [tempura](/wiki/Tempura "Tempura") and decoratively on platters of [sashimi](/wiki/Sashimi "Sashimi").[[23]](#cite_note-shoku-23)

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Ōtsu has 37 public elementary schools and 18 public middle schools operated by the city government and one private elementary school and four private middle schools. There are nine public high schools operated by the Shiga Prefectural Department of Education and three private high schools. The prefecture also operates three special education schools for the handicapped.

On April 1, 1963 [Shiga Prefectural Ishiyama High School](/wiki/Shiga_Prefectural_Ishiyama_High_School "Shiga Prefectural Ishiyama High School") was established.

International schools:
The city has a [North Korean school](/wiki/Chosun_gakko "Chosun gakko"), Shiga Korean Elementary School ([滋賀朝鮮初級学校](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%BB%8B%E8%B3%80%E6%9C%9D%E9%AE%AE%E5%88%9D%E7%B4%9A%E5%AD%A6%E6%A0%A1 "ja:滋賀朝鮮初級学校")).[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Railway")]

[Ōtsu Station](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu_Station "Ōtsu Station") is the central railroad station of the city, but the busiest station of the city is [Ishiyama Station](/wiki/Ishiyama_Station "Ishiyama Station"): 48 thousands users per a day as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit).[[25]](#cite_note-Traffic_Statistics_2009-25) Ōtsu and Ishiyama are major stations of the [West Japan Railway Company](/wiki/West_Japan_Railway_Company "West Japan Railway Company") (JR West) [Biwako Line](/wiki/Biwako_Line "Biwako Line"), a subsection of the [Tōkaidō Main Line](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Main_Line "Tōkaidō Main Line") that runs between [Maibara Station](/wiki/Maibara_Station "Maibara Station") and [Kyoto Station](/wiki/Kyoto_Station "Kyoto Station"). The [Keihan Electric Railway](/wiki/Keihan_Electric_Railway "Keihan Electric Railway") runs two [interurban](/wiki/Interurban "Interurban") lines, the [Keihan Keishin Line](/wiki/Keihan_Keishin_Line "Keihan Keishin Line") from Ōtsu to Kyoto, and the [Keihan Ishiyama Sakamoto Line](/wiki/Keihan_Ishiyama_Sakamoto_Line "Keihan Ishiyama Sakamoto Line"), which runs entirely within Ōtsu.[[26]](#cite_note-Kansai_Railway_Network_Map-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Line_and_Station_Guide-27) The [JR Central](/wiki/Central_Japan_Railway_Company "Central Japan Railway Company") [Tōkaidō Shinkansen](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Shinkansen "Tōkaidō Shinkansen") runs through areas of Ōtsu, but stops at no stations in the city.[[28]](#cite_note-shinkansen-28)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

Ōtsu is home to numerous museums. The [Shiga Prefectural Lake Biwa Culture Museum](/w/index.php?title=Shiga_Prefectural_Lake_Biwa_Culture_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shiga Prefectural Lake Biwa Culture Museum (page does not exist)"), founded in 1948, has exhibits on the culture of the Lake Biwa region.[[30]](#cite_note-Shiga_Prefectural_Lake_Biwa_Culture_Museum-30) The [Museum of Modern Art, Shiga](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art,_Shiga "Museum of Modern Art, Shiga"), was founded in 1984 and is located in the Setaminamigaya-chō district of the city.[[31]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Modern_Art,_Shiga-31) The [Ōtsu City Museum of History](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu_City_Museum_of_History&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtsu City Museum of History (page does not exist)") houses exhibits on the history of the city, as well as operating as a repository for cultural assets of Ōtsu. The museum is in the central Goryo-chō district directly north of Mii-dera.[[32]](#cite_note-About_the_Museum-32)

The city is home to two major libraries. The [Shiga Prefectural Library](/w/index.php?title=Shiga_Prefectural_Library&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shiga Prefectural Library (page does not exist)"), which houses approximately 1.2 million volumes, is located in the Setaminamigaya-chō district and operates as the central prefectural library. The library opened in 1943.[[33]](#cite_note-Timeline-33) The Ōtsu Municipal Library operates as a general public library for the city. The Municipal Library has a main building in the Hama-Ōtsu district, as well as three branch libraries and several [bookmobiles](/wiki/Bookmobile "Bookmobile").[[34]](#cite_note-shiga5-34)

Ōtsu is home to numerous historical sites, temples, shrines, and other buildings, many of them designated as [National Treasures of Japan](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan").

### Lake Biwa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Lake Biwa")]

Lake Biwa, the largest freshwater lake in Japan, covers 673.9 square kilometres (260.2 mi2) and is located at the center of the Shiga Prefecture.[[35]](#cite_note-biwa2-35) The north part of the lake reaches a depth of 50 metres (160 ft), and the south part of the lake near Ōtsu is much shallower and reaches a depth of 5 metres (16 ft). Lake Biwa provides water for the industrial areas of the Kansai Region, irrigation and drinking water in the Shiga area. The lake has been a travel destination since ancient times, and continues to support the tourism industry of the prefecture.[[36]](#cite_note-biwa-36) The lake is protected as part of [Biwako Quasi-National Park](/wiki/Biwako_Quasi-National_Park "Biwako Quasi-National Park").[[35]](#cite_note-biwa2-35) Lake Biwa is home to the [Lake Biwa Marathon](/wiki/Lake_Biwa_Marathon "Lake Biwa Marathon"), which started in Osaka in 1946, and moved to Lake Biwa in 1962. It is considered to be the oldest marathon in Japan.[[37]](#cite_note-marathon-37)

### Yodo River

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Yodo River")]

The [Yodo River](/wiki/Yodo_River "Yodo River") (120 kilometres (75 mi)) emerges from the south of Lake Biwa.[[38]](#cite_note-Seta_River-38) The portion of the river that emerges from the lake is called the Seta River; the portion of the river in Kyoto is referred to as the Uji River; and the portion in Osaka as the Yodo River. The [Setagawa Dam](/w/index.php?title=Setagawa_Dam&action=edit&redlink=1 "Setagawa Dam (page does not exist)") was constructed in 1961 to regulate the level of Lake Biwa, is located in the Nangō district of Ōtsu.[[39]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Seta_River-39) The Yodo River is noted for having the largest number of [tributaries](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of any river in Japan, and for supplying water for the [Hanshin Industrial Region](/wiki/Hanshin_Industrial_Region "Hanshin Industrial Region").[[40]](#cite_note-Yodogawa-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Yodo_River-41)

### Temples and shrines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Temples and shrines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref30s4592.jpg/250px-Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref30s4592.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref30s4592.jpg)

Mii-dera

Ōtsu is home to three temples with structures designated as [National Treasures](/wiki/List_of_National_Treasures_of_Japan_(temples) "List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)").

* [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") is a [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") is located on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") and overlooks Kyoto. The temple was founded by [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") (767 – 822), and remains both the headquarters of the Tendai sect and part of the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") "[Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)](/wiki/Historic_Monuments_of_Ancient_Kyoto_(Kyoto,_Uji_and_Otsu_Cities) "Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto (Kyoto, Uji and Otsu Cities)")".[[44]](#cite_note-Enryakuji-44)
* [Mii-dera](/wiki/Mii-dera "Mii-dera"), formally known as Onjō-ji, is the head temple of the [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") [Jimon](/wiki/Jimon_and_Sammon "Jimon and Sammon") sect. Mii-dera, which sits near the central area of the city, is one of the four largest temples in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"). It has 40 buildings within its sprawling temple precinct.[[45]](#cite_note-jkn217-45) [Ishiyama-dera](/wiki/Ishiyama-dera "Ishiyama-dera"), a [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon "Shingon") temple, was founded in 749 by the monk [Rōben](/wiki/R%C5%8Dben "Rōben") (689 – 773).
* Ishiyama-dera is traditionally thought to be the site where [Murasaki Shikibu](/wiki/Murasaki_Shikibu "Murasaki Shikibu") (c. 973 – c. 1014 or 1025) began writing *[The Tale of Genji](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Genji "The Tale of Genji")*. The temple is noted for its large collection of early Buddhist manuscripts.[[46]](#cite_note-Ishiyamadera-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* [Takebe taisha](/wiki/Takebe_taisha "Takebe taisha"), the *[ichinomiya](/wiki/Ichinomiya "Ichinomiya")* of former Ōmi Province
* [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha"), whose east and west main shrine buildings, the Nishi Hongū (西本宮) and Higashi Hongū (東本宮) are designated as [National Treasures](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan"),[[48]](#cite_note-Bunka2-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Bunka3-49) and many of the structures in the precincts are designated as National [Important Cultural Properties](/wiki/Important_Cultural_Property_(Japan) "Important Cultural Property (Japan)").

### Eight Views of Ōmi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Eight Views of Ōmi")]

The [Eight Views of Ōmi](/wiki/Eight_Views_of_%C5%8Cmi "Eight Views of Ōmi") refer to a series of scenic views of [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province"), the present-day [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"). The eight views were chosen in 1500 by a court noble and poet of the Muromachi period, [Konoe Masaie](/wiki/Konoe_Masaie "Konoe Masaie") (1444 – 1505). The views were inspired by the [Eight Views of Xiaoxiang](/wiki/Eight_Views_of_Xiaoxiang "Eight Views of Xiaoxiang"), China, and are located at the southern end of Lake Biwa. Six of the sites are within the city of Ōtsu, and two are located just beyond the borders of the city. The sights were depicted by [Hiroshige](/wiki/Hiroshige "Hiroshige") (1797 – 1858) in several different series of [ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") pictures, and served as an inspiration for other artists and literary figures.[[50]](#cite_note-The_Beauty_of_Shiga_Prefecture-50)[[51]](#cite_note-jkn218-51)

### Ōtsu Matsuri

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ōtsu Matsuri")]

The [Ōtsu Matsuri](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Ctsu_Matsuri&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōtsu Matsuri (page does not exist)") is the largest [festival](/wiki/Japanese_festivals "Japanese festivals") in the city. It begins Saturday, October 6 and ends on Sunday, October 7 and is connected to the [Tenson Shrine](/wiki/Tenson_Shrine "Tenson Shrine") in the Kyō-machi district of the city. The Ōtsu Matsuri is similar to the [Gion Matsuri](/wiki/Gion_Matsuri "Gion Matsuri") in Kyoto, and features thirteen tall lantern floats, which resemble those of the Gion. The floats feature *karakuri ningyō*, or mechanical dolls which operate via special mechanisms.[[52]](#cite_note-renmei-52) The thirteen floats each have their own tradition, customs, and lineage, and are paraded through the city from 9:30 am until 5 pm on the day of the festival.[[53]](#cite_note-biwako-visitors-53) The Ōtsu Matsuri is thought to have begun in the early Edo period, and the first written record of the festival dates to 1624. Many of the *hikiyama* in use today date from the Edo period, and are accompanied by *matsuri-bayashi* festival music unique to the city.[[52]](#cite_note-renmei-52) The Ōtsu Matsuri is designated a Prefectural Intangible Folk Treasure by Shiga Prefecture.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
